Escape will cancel and close the window. The University of New Hampshire nursing program is so popular this year that the school has closed undergraduate enrollment early. Officials said the deposit deadline for the fall semester is May 1, but the nursing program reached capacity last week. More than 2,000 students applied to the program, which has just 110 seats available, officials said. That number is a 10% increase in applications from last year. Officials said there are other options for students, such as pursuing a degree in public health or health sciences, but for now, they have to turn students away from the nursing program. >> Download the free WMUR app to get updates on the go << While that's not something the university said it likes to do, officials said the numbers are promising, given the current nursing shortage in New Hampshire. UNH plans to expand enrollment in the future. "We're going to hopefully continue our expansion to what we originally were projecting and then really work with the administration and work with admissions to have the right resources so that we can continue to provide the really great quality education that we've been giving the students," said Rhonda Board, interim director of the School of Nursing. The inaugural nursing class of 120 students will graduate next month. title: Fetty Wap Makes Waves at UNH - The New Hampshire
content: On Friday Apr. 10, UNH (University of New Hampshire) welcomed Fetty Wap to the Whittemore Arena to perform for a sold-out crowd. The show was put on by UNH’s student-run organization SCOPE (Student Committee on Popular Entertainment) and is their second concert of the school year, following last semester’s show featuring The Backseat Lovers. Doors opened at 6:30 p.m., and the crowd was ready to get into their seats and to the front of the barricade. The crowd started to flow into the Whittemore Center as excited fans who had been lined up and waiting for as long as two hours ran to get the best spot on the floor. SCOPE’s publicity director and UNH senior Sylvia Crawford says, “This year we saw the highest ticket sales since 2016.” There’s no question that UNH students and members of the surrounding community were excited and anticipatory about seeing Fetty Wap and supporting SCOPE as UNH’s largest outlet for live music and entertainment. Crawford also shared that SCOPE saw their demographic for ticket sales was close to an even split between UNH student tickets and public tickets. Setting the stage for Fetty Wap were two DJs, selected to open up the show after winning WUNH’s and SCOPE’s collaborative event, ‘Battle of the DJs’. First in the night's line-up was DJ Recon. Recon welcomed the arriving crowd in with an upbeat set, creating an electrifying atmosphere that set the tone for the rest of the night and hyped the concertgoers up to feel the excitement and anticipation in the arena. MEMi, the show’s second opener, followed up Recon’s set with a steady line-up of songs, jam-packed with 2000s throwbacks set to club beats that had everyone amped up and ready to welcome the night's headlining act onto the stage. Finally came the night’s main event as Fetty Wap took the stage donning a UNH Wildcats hockey jersey. The rapper came out a little after 9:00 p.m. to give the crowd a performance worth showing up for. He played hit tracks that were met with excited cheers from the audience and even brought a lucky group of concert-goers up onto the main stage to dance during a song. SCOPE’s spring show featuring Fetty Wap was one for the books. The event once again cements SCOPE as an organization that consistently seeks to go above and beyond for UNH students to experience entertainment like never before. We will see them yet again next semester for another exciting and eventful show.

title: Goldman Sachs Lifts UnitedHealth Target Price: Healthcare ETFs to Watch - The Globe and Mail
content: Investment banker Goldman Sachs recently raised its price target for UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) shares from $400 to $435, citing the company’s increased full-year guidance, announced in its latest quarterly results release, as evidence of UnitedHealth’s long-term earnings growth potential (as cited in Investing.com). Notably, Goldman Sachs expects UnitedHealth to return to at least 13% to 16% earnings per share growth over the next several years as it executes its business recovery. While this upgrade, following UNH’s recently released better-than-expected first-quarter 2026 results, may tempt investors to add the stock to their portfolios immediately, a significant headwind remains on the horizon. Legislative changes enacted last year have led to major funding cuts in Medicaid for 2026, putting UNH’s Medicaid business under intense margin pressure. In fact, the company’s management has already signaled expectations for membership attrition and negative margins within this specific segment throughout the year. However, for those who want to gain exposure to UnitedHealth’s growth while mitigating the idiosyncratic risks of its Medicaid business, diversified healthcare exchange-traded funds (ETFs) offer a more prudent path. By investing in funds where UNH is a top-10 holding, investors benefit from the company's strong medical cost management and declining medical care ratio (which improved to 83.9% in the first quarter), while spreading risk across other healthcare giants in pharma, biotech and medtech industries. Before suggesting a few such ETFs for your portfolio, let’s take a detailed look at UnitedHealth’s first-quarter performance across key metrics and how analysts from different firms reacted to this result to get a hint of the whole picture. UnitedHealth’s first-quarter adjusted earnings per share (EPS) surpassed the Zacks Consensus Estimate by 11.9%, while its revenues beat the consensus mark by 2.1%. On a year-over-year basis, the company delivered a solid performance on both counts. Its first-quarter operating cost ratio came in at 13.8%, up year over year from 12.4%, reflecting the timing of targeted investments across operations, technology and care delivery as well as incremental investments in areas such as AI, customer experience, cybersecurity and community engagement. UNH was successful in bringing its debt-to-capital ratio down to 42.9% by the end of the first quarter and remains on track to end this year with its goal of achieving a 40% debt-to-capital ratio. From the viewpoint of the share buyback program, the company initiated share repurchases earlier than anticipated and expects to deploy at least $2 billion by the end of the second quarter of 2026. As far as its Optum Rx business is concerned, UnitedHealth started 2026 by onboarding more than 800 new clients, while reducing contact call center volume 25% through enhanced digital and AI-enabled self-service, with member satisfaction reaching more than 95%. Within areas like consumer member experience, UNH recently launched Avery, a generative AI chatbot answering member questions for UnitedHealthcare. The company aims to expand this chatbot to include over 20 million members by the end of 2026. Meanwhile, Optum Real, an AI-first platform that UNH launched a couple of quarters ago, now has 0.5 billion transactions year to date, with the company expecting to close the year at over 2.5 billion transactions. Following UNH’s Q1 results, analysts from varied firms, apart from Goldman Sachs, have upgraded their price target for this stock, showing renewed optimism in its progress. For example, Morgan Stanley recently raised its price target on UNH from $375 to $395, while maintaining its “Overweight” rating. Other renowned institutions like Deutsche Bank raised its price target on UnitedHealth from $304 to $360, while maintaining a Hold rating, following the company’s first-quarter results. This fund, with net assets worth $738.8 million, provides exposure to 60 U.S. companies that provide health insurance, diagnostics and specialized treatment. Of these, UnitedHealth Group takes the first spot, accounting for a 26.50% share. CVS HealthCVS (12.49%) and HCA Healthcare (7.38%) hold the second and third positions in this fund, respectively. IHF charges 38 basis points (bps) in fees. It traded at a good volume of 1.24 million shares in the last trading session. It traded at a volume of 0.40 million shares in the last trading session. With net assets of $16.2 billion, this fund provides exposure to 407 companies engaged in health care equipment manufacturing, health care services, and the research, development, and marketing of pharmaceuticals and biotechnology products. Of these, UnitedHealth Group takes the fifth spot, accounting for a 3.94% share. LLY (11.89%), JNJ (9.34%) and ABBV (6.18%) hold the top three spots in this fund. VHT charges 9 bps in fees. title: 3 Healthcare Stocks Built for a Recession: UNH, LLY, ISRG - Gotrade
content: Healthcare is one of two sectors (along with consumer staples) that historically holds up best during US recessions. The reason is structural: medical demand is non-discretionary. People do not stop taking their diabetes medication because unemployment ticks up. Inside the sector, however, the defensive profile varies wildly. Insurers, hospital operators, drug distributors, and big pharma all have different exposures to government policy, commercial pricing, and patent cycles. Building a 3-stock recession-proof healthcare basket means picking names with the cleanest combination of recurring revenue, pricing power, and pipeline visibility. UNH, LLY, and ISRG each play a different role in the sector, each has a moat that does not depend on the next FDA decision or election, and each has produced strong long-term returns through the past three US economic cycles. During the 2008 recession, the S&P 500 fell roughly 38 percent peak to trough. The healthcare sector index fell about 23 percent. During COVID in March 2020, the S&P fell 34 percent and healthcare fell 27 percent. Healthcare does not avoid drawdowns, but it tends to recover faster because earnings are stickier. Drug consumption is non-cyclical. Surgical procedures get postponed but rarely cancelled. Insurance premiums get paid even when discretionary spending dries up. Total US health expenditure is now over 4.8 trillion dollars annually per CMS data, and the spending trajectory has compounded through every postwar recession. The full sector breakdown is laid out in our defensive stocks explainer, and the sector ETF context is in our healthcare ETF guide. UnitedHealth Group (UNH) is the largest US health insurer by membership, but the more interesting half of the company is Optum, the in-house pharmacy benefits manager and care services arm. Optum now generates more operating profit than the insurance business and grows faster. The combined entity collects premiums on roughly 50 million covered lives, and the cash flow profile is unusually defensive: premiums are paid monthly regardless of unemployment, and Medicare Advantage exposure has grown to over 7 million members. UNH has compounded earnings at over 12 percent annually for the past 15 years. Eli Lilly (LLY) is the rare healthcare name that combines defensive characteristics with growth-stock-like upside. Mounjaro and Zepbound, both based on the GLP-1 mechanism, have moved obesity from a chronic underserved condition into one of the largest pharmaceutical opportunities in history. Lilly's manufacturing capacity is the gating constraint, and the company has committed roughly 23 billion dollars in capex to expand fill-finish capacity through 2027. Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) makes the da Vinci surgical robot. The installed base is over 8,000 systems globally, and recurring revenue from instruments and accessories has grown to roughly 70 percent of total revenue. The model is razor-and-blade: hospitals buy the system once, then pay for sterile single-use instruments every procedure. Procedure volumes grow at low double-digits annually, even through recessions, because elective surgeries get delayed but not eliminated. An equal-weighted basket of UNH, LLY, and ISRG creates exposure to managed care, pharma, and medical devices in roughly equal proportions. Beta is below 1, dividend yield is modest (0.5 to 1.5 percent because most cash gets reinvested), and total return depends on earnings growth, not multiple expansion. Scale this basket at 5 to 10 percent of a US equity portfolio, paired with a sector ETF if you want broader exposure into biotech and tools. Healthcare is the defensive sector that does not feel defensive when you read the names. UnitedHealth is regulated insurance plus a fast-growing health services business. Eli Lilly is a defensive pharma with growth-stock upside from GLP-1. Intuitive Surgical is a recurring-revenue medical device platform with a 20-year data moat. Together they cover the three core profit pools of US healthcare and offer a real recession buffer. To set up the basket, allocate roughly equal positions across the three names in your Gotrade portfolio and check back semi-annually. ETFs like XLV give broad exposure but include slow-growth names that dilute the thesis. The 3-stock basket targets the highest-conviction sub-sectors with cleaner moats. For UNH, regulatory pressure on Medicare Advantage rate-setting. For LLY, GLP-1 competition. For ISRG, robotic surgery competitive entry from Medtronic and Johnson & Johnson. UNH and LLY pay modest dividends. ISRG does not pay one. The thesis here is total return, not income. How does this basket perform in a strong bull market? Healthcare typically lags in risk-on rallies, with returns driven more by individual catalyst news than the macro tape. title: UNH Stock Just Did Something Wall Street Didn’t See Coming - radiotandil.com
content: Right now, the first thing you notice about UNH is how subtly the stock has returned. A gradual rise to the mid-$350s is the type of price action that indicates large institutions are accumulating without wanting anyone to take a close look. It is neither a roar nor a celebration. This stock was trading above $438 a year ago. Then it broke. For a while, it seemed as though no one on Wall Street wanted to be caught holding it as it continued to crack, all the way down to $234 in the depths of last summer’s selloff. That has changed, albeit in a subtle way. Sanctuary Advisors increased its ownership by almost 11%. Nearly a million shares were added by Vanguard. Mattern Capital increased its stake by sixteen percent. By themselves, none of these actions are dramatic. When stacked together, they read like a silent endorsement from professionals who handle other people’s finances and would rather not telegraph their wagers. Determining whether this is a true recovery or merely a relief rally is more difficult. The bear case is still there. Every spring, Medicare Advantage rate-setting is reexamined as a political pressure point. The way moths find porch lights, pharmacy benefit managers—including Optum’s—continue to attract congressional attention. Last week, Robert W. Baird raised its target a notch but maintained its underperform rating. This type of conflicting coverage typically indicates that analysts themselves are unsure about what to do with the name. Nevertheless, the Optum division of the business continues to operate as it does. There were about fifty million covered lives. Medicare Advantage has more than seven million members. monthly premiums regardless of changes in the unemployment rate. It’s the type of cash flow profile that becomes dull until a recession strikes, at which point dullness becomes attractive. The announcement on April 24 that more than half of UNH’s volume would be standardized with electronic prior authorization, rising to 70% by year’s end, was the kind of operational housekeeping that doesn’t affect the stock on the day but has a long-term impact. The gap between sentiment and fundamentals is difficult to ignore. For fifteen years, the company’s compound annual growth rate of earnings has exceeded twelve percent. The dividend continues to rise. Free cash flow is consistent. Despite this, UNH is still trading about 20% below its 52-week high, and the majority of analysts are holding their ratings while they wait for someone else to make a move. It’s really unclear if this is the bottom or merely a pause. $400 is seen by Royal Bank of Canada. TD Cowen is nearer $337. Both cannot be correct, and the spread reveals how unsure even experts are. For the time being, UNH has returned to discussions from which it had been discreetly excluded. That is a form of recovery in and of itself. title: Is UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) One of the Best Low Volatility Stocks to Invest in Right Now? - Insider Monkey
content: UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) is one of the best low volatility stocks to invest in right now. On April 23, TD Cowen lifted the price target on UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) to $337 from $311, maintaining a Hold rating on the shares and noting that it reported better than expected results. The firm thus raised estimates and multiples to bring to light an improved view of both FY26 and FY27 margin recovery. In a separate development, UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) announced on April 24 that over half of its previous authorization volume will be included in an industry-wide effort to standardize submission requirements for electronic prior authorization. It added that the number will advance to over 70% by the end of 2026, and marks the next phase of the company’s efforts to modernize and simplify prior authorization for care providers. It is also aimed at helping patients access safe and evidence-based care more quickly and easily, while simultaneously preserving important clinical safeguards. UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) stated that the new standardized documentation process will apply across the company’s commercial, Medicare Advantage, and Medicaid offerings. title: “So if I want to get insurance payouts, I have to go to a hospital affiliated with the insurer?”In D.. - 매일경제
content:
“So if I want to get insurance payouts, I have to go to a hospital affiliated with the insurer?” In December 2024, Brian Thompson, the CEO of UnitedHealthcare, the largest health insurer in the United States, was shot and killed in broad daylight in downtown New York. A bullet found at the scene reportedly bore words that evoked a denial of insurance claims. Let’s look at UnitedHealth Group (UNH)’s annual business report to understand why such an incident may have happened. First, the business structure shows that UNH is divided into two divisions: health insurance, through UnitedHealthcare, and health care services, through Optum. Optum is further split into Optum Health, which operates hospitals and treats patients; OptumRx, which manages prescriptions; and Optum Insight, an IT company that analyzes medical data. Since 2011, UNH has aggressively acquired hospitals and pharmacies, moving toward vertical integration. With everything from treatment and prescriptions to insurance handled inside the company, less money has had to flow outside. As other insurers watched this strategy, many began to follow UNH’s lead, and in the U.S. it became normal for policyholders to lose insurance coverage if they did not use company-affiliated hospitals. As a result, whether an insurer owns many hospitals and pharmacies became an important factor in choosing an insurance company. UNH, the early leader, quickly gained an edge and was able to build a Value-based care model. Under this approach, insurance premiums collected from customers are used to keep them healthy and prevent serious illness. If preventive care helps avoid major disease, the hospital costs saved become profit for the insurer. It is the opposite of the traditional hospital model, where revenue rises when patients visit more often. Despite its extensive health care services, UNH’s notoriety kept growing. That was because it failed to pay claims properly. In general, health insurers take the premiums they collect, subtract hospital costs, and keep the remainder. To increase profits, they need to maintain an appropriate payout ratio while expanding their customer base. But UNH chose a different path. It began maximizing corporate profit by lowering its claim payout ratio itself. According to insurance research firm Value Penguin, UNH’s claim denial rate stands at 33%, the highest in the industry. Many policyholders lost the chance to receive treatment because they could not get insurance payouts, and that ultimately led to the CEO shooting incident. UNH’s stock, which hovered around $500 in early 2025, also plunged sharply to around $250. But the stock’s drop by half cannot be explained by the shooting alone. The business report shows that company profits also fell sharply throughout 2025. What caused it? One major factor is the steep rise in the medical loss ratio, or MCR. MCR is an indicator of how much of the premiums an insurer collects are spent on patients’ hospital bills. Last year, UNH’s MCR was 89.1%, meaning that for every $100 in premiums, more than $89 went to hospital expenses. The problem is that this figure has been rising steadily for several years. There are many reasons for the surge in medical costs. First, people have been visiting hospitals more often, and the cost of treatment has also gone up. In addition, more patients are receiving more complex and precise tests during each visit. On top of that, its core pharmacy services business, OptumRx, is also under pressure. The U.S. drug pricing system includes a structure known as a kickback. When pharmaceutical companies offer discounts to PBM firms in order to promote their drugs, the PBMs place those drugs at the top of their recommendation lists. Recently, the U.S. government has been tightening regulations, saying that kickbacks drive up drug prices. Under the 2026 CAA legislation passed earlier this year, PBMs must pass all kickbacks they receive from drugmakers on to consumers. In addition, amid antitrust investigations, cyberattacks, and other pressures, UNH’s operating profit for 2025 fell 41.3% from a year earlier to $18.964 billion. In response, Stephen Hemsley, chairman of UNH, said at the company’s March earnings release that it would overcome market pressure through AI-based productivity innovation and a restructuring of its business model. title: UNH Athletic Director Allison Rich Will Not Return Next School Year - WMUR
content: University of New Hampshire Athletic Director Dr. Allison Rich’s contract will expire in July and she will not return, a school spokesperson confirmed to WMUR. Rich has been the AD at UNH since 2022. She also works on the NCAA’s Division I Council and is a chairperson on the NCAA’s Infractions Appeals Committee. Prior to her work in Durham, she worked at Princeton, where she was an undergraduate. Rich was the Tigers’ Senior Associate Director of Athletics for nine years. The school says a search committee will be formed to find the next Wildcats’ athletic director. title: Why UnitedHealth Group (UNH) Is Up 9.3% After Q1 Beat, Raised Guidance And AI Push – And What's Next - simplywall.st
content:
Outshine the giants: these 19 early-stage AI stocks could fund your retirement. To own UnitedHealth Group, you need to believe its integrated insurance and Optum platforms can translate stable cash generation and disciplined medical cost management into reliable long term earnings, even after a difficult 2025. The latest earnings beat and higher EPS guidance support that thesis, but they do not remove the key short term risk: further shocks in Medicare-related utilization and policy, particularly as funding and risk-model changes continue to work through the system. The most relevant recent announcement is UnitedHealth’s plan to invest about US$1.5 billion in AI initiatives in 2026, including tools like generative AI chatbot Avery and the Optum Real platform. These projects are already being tied to improved medical cost ratios and operational efficiency, which matters directly to the current catalyst of margin repair. If they scale as intended, they could partially offset pressures from volatile Medicare utilization and reimbursement. Yet beneath the improving headlines, investors should be aware of mounting regulatory and policy scrutiny that could materially affect UnitedHealth’s margins and pricing power over time... Read the full narrative on UnitedHealth Group (it's free!) UnitedHealth Group's narrative projects $501.1 billion revenue and $20.0 billion earnings by 2028. This requires 5.8% yearly revenue growth and a $1.3 billion earnings decrease from $21.3 billion today. Uncover how UnitedHealth Group's forecasts yield a $364.62 fair value, a 3% upside to its current price. Before this quarter’s surprise, the most pessimistic analysts were assuming revenue of about US$468.6 billion and earnings of US$15.8 billion by 2028, which bakes in sustained margin pressure from government scrutiny and policy changes. Compared with the more constructive consensus view tied to AI efficiencies and margin recovery, that is a much harsher story, and it shows how differently you and other shareholders can read the same business, especially as new results and guidance emerge. title: Maine women set to open America East tournament vs UNH on Thursday - foxbangor.com
content: ORONO -- Momentum is one of the driving forces behind any great playoff run, and the Maine women’s basketball team certainly has it on their side as they enter the America East tournament as the conference's number 2 seed. The Black Bears earned that spot with two big wins last week vs Albany and Binghamton. And with the regular season winding down, coaches and players understood what was at stake. “It was just about finishing on a high note," said senior Adrianna Smith. "We know what the stakes were, but we also knew, it’s a basketball game, we have to get ready for playoffs, get polished up, and we were able to do that. To me, it felt like Saturday was a championship or playoff vibe.” “Obviously we know we have later games in the week, but we have to focus on what’s right in front of us and take it one play at a time, and one game at a time," said senior Sarah Talon. LATEST VIDEOSLocking up the two seed ensures the Black Bears a home playoff game in the opening round, an advantage they know is huge for the team in an elimination game. “It just makes a big difference," said Smith. "Our last three games have been great games, really competitive, high intensity. I really think that we were able to pull them all off because they were at home and we were able to have that atmosphere.” “It doesn’t go unnoticed with the people in the crowd," said Talon. "It’s a great feeling to have them support you. Having them here, it helps us defensively and gets us going.” That crowd will surely be rowdy in Orono on Thursday, when the Black Bears take on perennial rival UNH in the first round. After two matchups with the Wildcats this season, Maine knows what it will take to get the job done. “They’ve been playing really well," said head coach Amy Vachon. "When they played here, they had a lot of other kids step up and hit shots. We will have to lock in and play a total team defensive game.” “As long as we show up and do what we need to do, we will be ok," said Talon. "But we need to focus on what we can control.” “They have a lot of great scorers and great action, but this is our third time playing them," said Smith. "We know what they look like, they know what we look like. In the playoffs, it just comes down to who wants it more.” Maine's opening round matchup with UNH tips off in Orono on Thursday at 6PM.
